description: The module implements the PCI Express protocol as described in the architecture chapter. It covers the structural organization of the controller and its role within the KeyStone system. Transaction, data link, and physical layer behaviors are defined at a system level. Industry standards compliance is stated for interoperability. Integration notes describe how this peripheral connects to the SoC fabric.
Remarks: Follow the stated architectural model and standards alignment during system integration.

description: Clock control for the PCIe subsystem is defined to ensure proper enablement before link activities. Software must turn on the module clock domain through the device power/clock controller. The procedure is part of the prescribed initialization flow. Improper sequencing can prevent link bring-up. Guidance is provided on where to control these settings in the SoC.
Remarks: Ensure clocks are stable before enabling link training or accessing PCIe registers.

description: The SerDes must be configured with the proper PLL settings prior to enabling the link. Software programs the SerDes configuration register and enables the PLL. A lock indicator bit reports when the PLL has stabilized. The reference clock must be present and valid. This step is mandatory for any subsequent link bring-up.
dependencies: Valid reference clock; SerDes configuration; PLL enable
Remarks: Poll the LOCK status before proceeding to LTSSM enable to avoid failed training.

description: The controller supports operation as a Root Complex with a defined initialization sequence. Software sets the module enable and selects RC mode in the device status register. Configuration registers are programmed before starting link training. After training, software performs bus enumeration and downstream configuration. Final steps include establishing DMA and interrupt procedures with endpoints.
dependencies: PSC enable; DEVSTAT mode select; SerDes PLL lock; software enumeration
Remarks: Adhere to the step-by-step RC sequence to ensure reliable discovery and configuration of downstream devices.

description: Link training is controlled through a command/status register bit that enables the LTSSM. Hardware resets de-assert the training enable, requiring software to assert it when ready. A debug register exposes the current LTSSM state for progress verification. Successful training is indicated by a specific state value. This enables transition to higher-level configuration and traffic.
Remarks: Defer LTSSM enable until all clocking and configuration are complete to avoid repeated retraining.

description: The module provides programmable address translation to map system addresses into PCIe regions. This allows software to control how local memory is exposed to PCIe transactions. Proper setup is required for both inbound and outbound accesses. The mechanism is described alongside the architectural address mapping. Correct configuration is essential for DMA and memory transactions to target the intended spaces.
dependencies: System memory map; translation window programming
Remarks: Define translation windows early in initialization to avoid unintended accesses during enumeration or DMA.

feature_name: PCIe address spaces and configuration space
description: The controller defines the organization of PCIe address spaces including configuration access. A fixed-size configuration space is described with stable register offsets independent of access mode. This provides a consistent view for header and capability structures. Software must use these spaces for standard PCIe configuration procedures. The mapping details are essential for compatibility with typical enumeration flows.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Ch 2.8; p.2-19 (1 KB configuration space)
Remarks: Use the documented offsets when accessing configuration headers to ensure predictable behavior across modes.

description: The architecture provides support for performing DMA data movement across the PCIe link. Guidance is given for coordinating with system software to establish DMA protocols. After link and configuration, endpoints and the host coordinate DMA transfers. Proper address translation and completion handling are prerequisites. This enables high-throughput memory transactions over PCIe.
dependencies: System DMA engine; address translation; interrupt procedures
Remarks: Validate DMA paths with small transfers before enabling sustained traffic to confirm translations and completions.

description: The module handles standard PCIe transactions as described in the transactions section. Memory and configuration operations are supported per the architecture. Ordering and completion behaviors follow the PCIe model. Software must configure windows and headers to enable these flows. This underpins both control and bulk data movements.
Remarks: Ensure transaction types match the configured windows to prevent unsupported requests or errors.

description: A built-in loopback capability allows validation of the PCIe link without external traffic. This is useful during bring-up and self-test scenarios. Software enables loopback through documented procedures. Observing LTSSM and status indicators verifies integrity. It provides a controlled environment for link quality checks.
Remarks: Disable normal traffic during loopback tests and restore standard settings before resuming operation.

description: The controller integrates interrupt support as part of its architecture. The section outlines how interrupts are generated and handled. This includes their role during initialization and runtime error reporting. Proper configuration is needed to route and enable them. Interrupts are essential for efficient event-driven control.
dependencies: System interrupt controller configuration; PCIe configuration
Remarks: Map interrupts early and verify handling paths before enabling DMA or advanced features.

description: Message Signaled Interrupts are supported through dedicated capability registers. Software programs these registers to advertise and generate MSI events. This enables in-band signaling without dedicated pins. Proper setup aligns with the system interrupt handling. MSI is commonly used for PCIe device notifications.
dependencies: MSI capability enable; system interrupt routing
Remarks: Use MSI for scalable interrupt delivery and verify message vectors during device enumeration.

description: Power management features are described along with their control registers. Software can configure link and function-level power policies through the documented capability set. This helps manage power during idle or low-activity periods. Transitions require coordination with link state and activity. Proper configuration improves energy efficiency without sacrificing responsiveness.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Ch 2.15; Ch 3.5 (Power Management Capability Registers)
dependencies: Clocks and link state coordination; capability programming
Remarks: Test low-power transitions under representative load to confirm wake timing and state retention.

description: The module includes mechanisms for detecting and handling errors during PCIe operation. The section explains how errors are surfaced to software. These events can be tied into the interrupt framework. Correct handling prevents system stalls and aids recovery. Monitoring aids in diagnosing link or protocol issues.
Remarks: Log and clear error status promptly and consider backoff or retraining upon recurring faults.

description: Standard PCIe capability structures are implemented and described via their registers. Extended capabilities are also available and documented in a dedicated section. Software reads and configures these to enable optional behaviors. Proper setup ensures compliance and feature negotiation. Port logic registers complement capability control for link operation.
evidence: SPRUGS6D Ch 3.7 (PCIe Capabilities); Ch 3.8 (Extended Capabilities); Ch 3.9 (Port Logic)
Remarks: Probe capabilities during enumeration and only enable options confirmed by both link partners.

description: Emulation-related guidance is provided to support development and debug. The section outlines considerations when the system is under emulation control. This includes any interactions with the PCIe module during halt or step operations. Awareness of these behaviors prevents unintended link disruptions. It is useful during bring-up and diagnostics.
Remarks: Coordinate with emulation tools to maintain link stability when halting cores or inspecting registers.

description: Signal descriptions and pin multiplexing information specify how the PCIe interface is brought out on the device pins. This determines the electrical connections and alternative functions. Proper selection is required to enable PCIe mode on the desired pins. The documentation guides these choices. Correct pinmux is a prerequisite for successful link operation.
dependencies: Device I/O mux configuration; board-level connections
Remarks: Verify board routing and pinmux tables to ensure the SerDes lanes and control pins are correctly assigned.

description: Reset behavior for the PCIe subsystem is documented to aid robust initialization and recovery. The section covers module resets and their effects on state. Software must sequence resets in coordination with clocks and SerDes. Proper handling ensures a clean starting point for link training. It also assists in error recovery scenarios.
Remarks: Issue resets judiciously and re-run the full initialization sequence after reset de-assertion.

description: The controller exposes standard configuration header registers for device and bridge roles. Common fields and type-specific registers are documented. These are used during enumeration and resource assignment. Software must program and read these consistently. Stable offsets are provided for reliable access.
Remarks: Use the documented header layout to allocate BARs and set command/status bits accurately.}
